text: Which of the following data sources is part of the TAF's OWN logging, rather than a log produced by the System Under Test?

options.text: Web server access logs generated by the SUT's web tier.
Database server transaction logs generated by the SUT's persistence layer.
TAF audit trail logs recording which test steps were executed and their outcomes.
Application performance metrics collected by a production monitoring tool.

explanation: The TAF produces its own audit trail logs that record the execution of test steps, their outcomes, and timestamps. This is distinct from logs produced by the SUT (application logs, web server logs, database logs) or production monitoring systems. Having clear TAF logs is essential for distinguishing automation failures from SUT defects.

text: When analysing a failed automated test result, what is the CORRECT sequence of steps to determine whether the failure is a SUT defect or a TAS defect?

options.text: Log a defect immediately, then investigate the root cause after the release.
Check whether the same failure occurred in previous runs, identify the failing test step, analyse the actual vs expected state, then log a defect if the SUT is at fault.
Rerun the test multiple times and log a defect only if it fails three times in a row.
Delete the failing test and recreate it from scratch before investigating.

explanation: The correct analysis sequence is: (1) check if the same failure occurred in previous runs (is this a regression?), (2) identify which test case is failing, (3) pinpoint the failing test step, (4) compare actual vs expected state to determine root cause, (5) if the SUT behaviour is incorrect, log a defect. If the TAS is at fault (e.g., flawed assertion or script error), it is a TAS defect, not a SUT defect.

text: An automated test fails, but investigation reveals the SUT was behaving correctly. The test failure was caused by an incorrect assertion in the test script itself. How should this be classified?

options.text: A SUT defect, because any test failure indicates a product problem.
An environment outage, because the test environment was not stable.
A TAS defect, because the failure originates from a problem in the test automation solution, not the SUT.
A flaky test, because it failed once but the SUT is fine.

explanation: When a test failure is caused by an error in the test automation solution itself (such as an incorrect assertion, flawed test logic, or a bug in the TAF) rather than by incorrect SUT behaviour, it is classified as a TAS (Test Automation Solution) defect. This distinction is critical for correct defect reporting and avoiding false bug reports against the SUT.

text: Which type of stakeholder for test progress reporting is MOST interested in high-level trend data such as the overall pass/fail ratio and test execution reliability over multiple sprints?

options.text: Technical stakeholders such as test automation engineers who need low-level step-by-step logs.
Operational stakeholders focused on how the product is being used in production.
Management stakeholders who need high-level trends, pass-fail ratios, and reliability indicators to make release decisions.
End users of the SUT who want to know when new features will be delivered.

explanation: Management stakeholders require high-level test progress information: trends over time, overall pass/fail ratios, and test suite reliability. This information supports release readiness decisions. Technical stakeholders need detailed, low-level logs. Operational stakeholders are concerned with production usage metrics. End users are not typically consumers of test progress reports.

text: Which of the following is a feature of the Test Automation Solution that DIRECTLY supports the measurement of test execution results?

options.text: Choosing a programming language that matches the SUT's technology stack.
Assertions in test scripts that evaluate actual vs expected outcomes to determine pass or fail status.
Using pair programming when writing test scripts.
Maintaining a branching strategy in version control.

explanation: Assertions are the primary mechanism by which automated tests determine pass or fail status. They compare the actual state of the SUT against the expected state and record the result. Without assertions, tests produce no meaningful measurement of SUT behaviour. Pre/during/post execution logging, historical trend tracking, and assertion design are all features that support effective test measurement.

text: Which of the following methods can be used to PUBLISH test progress reports to the relevant stakeholders?

options.text: Printing physical copies and distributing them at weekly meetings only.
Publishing via a dedicated reporting website, cloud-based dashboards, email notifications, or chatbot integrations.
Storing reports only on the test engineer's local machine.
Only sharing reports directly in code review pull requests.

explanation: Test progress reports can be published through multiple channels: dedicated reporting websites, cloud-based dashboard tools (e.g., TestRail, Allure, Azure DevOps), email notifications, and chatbot integrations (e.g., Slack or Teams notifications). The goal is to make reports accessible to the appropriate stakeholders in the format and channel most useful to them.

text: You are working on a test automation project used to automate GUI testing of an online web shop wizard. Screenshots were taken during recording and stored as a baseline. The baseline was rerun with no SUT change and all tests passed. After developers submitted a change to the wizard, the recorded scripts were played back and screenshots compared with the baseline. During playback, all test cases failed even though the visible content seems unchanged. What could be the cause?

options.text: An internally used technical session ID is also recorded, which changes during the playback. This should be substituted with variables to avoid failures.
Screenshots are not correctly associated with the test steps, leading to comparisons being made in the wrong order.
The failure occurred because relying solely on screenshot comparisons led to inaccurate results.
The date in the GUI header is different from the date when it was recorded. This date field should be excluded from the comparison.
Screenshots were captured in low resolution, causing details to be blurred, making the comparison unreliable.

explanation: Session IDs change with each session, causing screenshot differences even when visible content looks the same. Dynamic elements like dates in the GUI header can cause false negatives and should be excluded from screenshot comparisons. Order of screenshots is not relevant since all tests fail. The failure is not due to using screenshot comparisons per se. Low resolution is unlikely to cause all failures if content appears identical to the human eye.

text: You are extending a TAF with additional test logging capabilities. The TAF uses a third-party tool to create file logs and an HTML report. Various dynamic values are used to generate test data, and the SUT is a web service without a UI that connects to multiple legacy test systems. The tests are very unstable, and you need to add meaningful information to the test logging to better analyze failure reasons. Which of the following should NOT be included in the test logging?

options.text: Timestamps, to see if the failure connects to a given legacy system outage.
Screenshots, to see actual request-response pairs.
Randomly generated values, to allow analysis of the actual results occurring during test executions.
On assertion failures, actual results versus expected results instead of stack traces.

explanation: The SUT is a web service without a UI, so screenshots are irrelevant and should NOT be included. Logging request and response data is more useful for analyzing web service interactions. Timestamps help identify connections to legacy service outages. Logging randomly generated values allows for later investigation. Focused assertion messages aid investigation of failures.

text: During the design phase of a performance critical software product, management decides to create a TAS to do load testing on the software to measure its performance. The product contains different architectural components including a browser-based front end, microservices in the back end, and a relational database. It is important to measure all individual transactions in the entire architectural stack. How can you automatically provide this information from the test automation software?

options.text: You cannot. This information has to be gathered manually at the end of every transaction.
The test automation engineer (TAE) can record the timing information during the execution of all transactions.
Trace IDs should be populated across the software components and measured time values should be associated with these IDs to correlate the performance data.
Third-party tools should be inserted into the different layers of the architecture and these agents should log the collected data into a database.

explanation: Trace IDs are the appropriate way to associate performance data across different architectural components, enabling system-wide performance analysis and correlation. Manual data collection is too time-consuming and error-prone. Recording timing in the TAE alone does not explain how data can be correlated across different components. Third-party tools can collect data but do not provide the necessary correlation for complete analysis.

text: As a test automation engineer, you are tasked with demonstrating to management whether the test success rate is improving over a series of test runs. What is the right approach to fulfill this requirement?

options.text: Compare the test results with the expected results.
Use traffic light indicators to show test execution progress.
Produce detailed test reports with percentages of test completion.
Implement an analysis feature that compares previous test results and highlights trends.

explanation: Implementing an analysis feature that compares previous test run results and highlights trends is the correct approach for demonstrating improvement in test success rate over a series of runs. Comparing test results with expected results only gives the pass/fail rate for one test run. Traffic lights only show the summary of progress within one test run cycle. Detailed reports with percentages only show outcome within one test run cycle.

text: You have created a test automation suite that is testing critical functionality in a new system. It is finding a significant number of defects. You have repeatedly shown your output logs to your manager, but he is not understanding that most of the test cases are failing. What should you do to enhance your logs to make them more understandable?

options.text: Nothing. Get a new manager.
Ensure the start and end of every test is logged and presented in bold font
Add color to the results showing red for failures and green for passes
Add screen shots for all the failures

explanation: Adding color to results (red for failures, green for passes) will help make it obvious to management that there are many failures. A summary at the beginning would also be helpful. Bold start/end logging (B) should already be done but won't help the manager understand the logs. Screenshots (D) would help developers, not the manager.

text: You have a test automation suite that is encountering a failure. You have investigated the logs, but you can't see where it's failing. You've tried running the tests manually and they work without a problem. What other information should you capture in your scripts to help debug the problem?

options.text: Screen shots at the point of the failure
Lines of code executed
Equivalent manual testing hours saved
Number of executions with pass/fail information

explanation: Screenshots at the point of failure provide visual evidence of the SUT state at the moment the automation fails. Since manual tests pass but automated tests fail, visual capture helps identify UI state differences or timing issues that are not apparent from text logs alone.

text:
You are working on a Customer Relationship Management (CRM) system that uses a third-party address lookup to help the user when they enter an address. This also ensures that only valid addresses are entered into the system. Unfortunately, you are finding that addresses in one state are always failing — Kansas. You can't see any problems in the test automation code, and you have determined that there are no data or formatting errors in the failing addresses. You have tried the same test manually and you get the same failure.

Given this information, what should you do next?

options.text: You should run the code under the debugger to be sure there are no errors occurring
You should contact the third-party vendor and log an incident with information regarding the failed addresses
You should capture screen shots of the failure
You should ensure that the addresses being entered are valid addresses

explanation: You have already verified that the TAS and SUT are working correctly, and that the data being passed is correct. Since the same failure occurs manually, it is likely the problem is with the third-party address lookup service. Contacting the vendor and logging an incident is the appropriate next step.

text: Who should receive a copy of a test progress report?

options.text: Only the testers until the software is ready to release
Management, but only if there is an issue they need to resolve
All stakeholders who have a need and/or interest in the testing
No one - this information should only be tracked in a dashboard that is accessible to the technical team members

explanation: Test progress reports should be distributed to all stakeholders who have a need or interest in the testing progress. This includes management, developers, business representatives, and other relevant parties — not just the test team.
